title: Comment on "Association between quantum paradoxes based on weak values and a realistic interpretation of quantum measurements"
summary_bullets: Authors show that Aredes and Saldanha’s “General Argument” against realistic interpretations of weak values is formally incorrect and not generally applicable. Using Bohmian mechanics as a counterexample, they demonstrate that weak values postselected in position coincide with Bohmian local expectation values (e.g., position, velocity, energy) and can be interpreted as intrinsic properties of systems detached from measuring devices. They further argue many paradoxes vanish under this interpretation, clarify the role of contextuality and measurement backaction, and provide explicit examples (velocity operator, three-box paradox).

Why Yanbei would care: the paper speaks directly to quantum measurement foundations and to the empirical meaning of weak-value estimates — quantities that are used as diagnostic tools in precision measurement and in reconstructing quantum trajectories, areas closely tied to Yanbei’s work on quantum measurement theory, back-action evasion, and optomechanics. By showing when weak values can be treated as real system properties versus measurement-context artifacts, it impacts how one should design and interpret weak-measurement protocols in tabletop tests of macroscopic quantum behavior and any metrology aiming to detect subtle effects (including possible gravity-related signatures).

Keywords/topics: weak values, Bohmian mechanics (de Broglie–Bohm), weak measurement and postselection, local expectation values, contextuality, quantum measurement foundations, experimental weak-value protocols, relevance to optomechanics and precision metrology; arXiv:2603.18713, published 19 Mar 2026 (very recent).
